title: Hameldown Hampden Memorial
crew_summary: 4 killed (Wilson, Lyon, Branes, Ellis); returning from operations over France
memorial_type: Granite boulder incised with initials and squadron number; plaque added 1991
narrative: On the night of 21 March 1941, six Hampden bombers of 49 Squadron lifted from RAF Scampton and set course for the German naval base at Lorient in occupied France. On return, X3054 experienced a radio malfunction that rendered it unable to receive signals. Without functioning radio aids, the pilot -- 25-year-old Pilot Officer Robert Wilson -- became disoriented regarding his position relative to the English coast. Flying in deteriorating visibility, the Hampden struck the western slope of Hameldown Tor at approximately 1,400 feet and burst into flames. Sergeants Brames, Ellis, and Lyon appear to have died on impact, but Wilson -- strapped into the pilot's seat -- survived initially, only to die the following day. Wilson and Ellis lie side by side in Exeter's Higher Cemetery; the two air gunners were buried in their home towns. All four were 25 or under. The Hampden was one of the earlier Bomber Command types, a cramped twin-engine aircraft with a crew of four, already being superseded by the heavier Halifax and Lancaster by 1941.

crew.name: Robert David Wilson
crew.fate_detail: Survived the initial impact but died the following day from his injuries. Buried side by side with Sgt Ellis at Exeter Higher Cemetery.
crew.bio: A 25-year-old pilot with 49 Squadron at RAF Scampton. Wilson survived the crash initially but succumbed to his injuries the next day.

crew.name: Richard Leonard Ashburton Ellis
crew.fate_detail: Killed on impact when the Hampden struck Hameldown Tor. Buried beside his pilot P/O Wilson at Exeter Higher Cemetery.

crew.name: Ronald Brames
crew.burial_location: Eltham (St John the Baptist) Churchyard, London
crew.fate_detail: Killed on impact at Hameldown Tor. Buried in his home town at Eltham churchyard in London.

crew.name: Charles John Lyon
crew.fate_detail: Killed on impact at Hameldown Tor. Buried in his home area at Prescot Cemetery in Lancashire.

cause_of_loss: Radio unable to receive; pilot disoriented regarding coastal position; flew into high ground in poor visibility
operation_target: Operational -- returning from bombing raid on Lorient, France
memorial_details: The stone memorial on Hameldown Tor was commissioned by Lady Marjorie Cecilia Wilson, mother of the pilot. The north face is dated 21.3.41 and bears the incised initials of all four crew members beneath a cross. The memorial was extensively restored in 1991 by the Torbay and District Aircrew Association. A Remembrance Day ceremony is held at the site periodically. The memorial is accessible via the Two Moors Way footpath across Dartmoor, though it requires a moorland walk of approximately 2 miles from the nearest road.

maintainer.notes: Granite boulder incised with initials and squadron number; plaque added 1991.

external_narratives.summary: The 49 Squadron Association maintains a dedicated page for the Hameldown Tor memorial. It records that six Hampdens departed Scampton at 18:20 on 21 March 1941 to bomb the submarine base at Lorient, and that X3054's crew lost their radio fix on return, striking the hillside at about 1,400 feet.
key_details: Squadron Association maintains active memorial page

external_narratives.summary: Detailed walking guide to the crash site with historical context. Describes the granite boulder memorial commissioned by Lady Wilson and its 1991 restoration by the Torbay and District Aircrew Association.
Listed as '49 Sqn RAF Crash Site' in the War Memorials Online register, confirming the memorial's status and location on Hameldown Tor, Dartmoor.
The Imperial War Museum records 49 Squadron RAF in its memorials register, linking to the Hameldown site among the squadron's commemorative locations.

The Commonwealth War Graves Commission confirms that Pilot Officer Wilson and Sergeant Ellis are buried at Exeter Higher Cemetery. The CWGC maintains their graves as part of the 73 Commonwealth war burials at the site.
key_details: Wilson and Ellis confirmed buried at Exeter Higher Cemetery
73 Commonwealth war burials at this cemetery
